constraints.text: An object may have multiple resources that clients can use to perform specific actions that create, update, delete, or get.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_001; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The name of a list kind must end with "List".
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_002; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All lists use the required "items" field to contain the array of objects they return.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_003; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Any kind that has the "items" field must be a list kind.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_004; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Most objects defined in the system should have an endpoint that returns the full set of resources, as well as zero or more endpoints that return subsets of the full list.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_005; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some objects may be singletons (the current user, the system defaults) and may not have lists.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_006; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In addition, all lists that return objects with labels should support label filtering (see the labels documentation), and most lists should support filtering by fields (see the fields documentation).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_007; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When resources wish to expose alternative actions or views that are closely coupled to a single resource, they should do so using new sub-resources.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_008; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: `/status`: Used to write just the `status` portion of a resource. For example, the `/pods` endpoint only allows updates to `metadata` and `spec`, since those reflect end-user intent. An automated process should be able to modify status for users to see by sending an updated Pod kind to the server to the "/pods/<name>/status" endpoint - the alternate endpoint allows different rules to be applied to the update, and access to be appropriately restricted.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_009; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=bullet

constraints.text: The standard REST verbs (defined below) MUST return singular JSON objects.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_010; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some API endpoints may deviate from the strict REST pattern and return resources that are not singular JSON objects, such as streams of JSON objects or unstructured text log data.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_011; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: These types may evolve independent of the API group that uses them and API servers may allow them to be addressed in their generic form.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_012; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The term "type" should be used for distinguishing sub-categories within objects or subobjects.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_013; review=kept; section=Types (Kinds); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All JSON objects returned by an API MUST include a `kind` field that identifies the schema this object should have.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_014; review=rewritten; section=Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=217-217

constraints.text: All JSON objects returned by an API MUST include an `apiVersion` field that identifies the version of the schema this object should have.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_015; review=rewritten; section=Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=217-217

constraints.text: Every object kind MUST have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": namespace: a namespace is a DNS compatible label that objects are subdivided into. The default namespace is 'default'. See the namespace docs for more.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_018; review=kept; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=231-232

constraints.text: Every object kind MUST have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": name: a string that uniquely identifies this object within the current namespace (see the identifiers docs). This value is used in the path when retrieving an individual object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_019; review=kept; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=231-232

constraints.text: Every object kind MUST have a `metadata.uid` field that uniquely distinguishes objects with the same name that have been deleted and recreated.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_020; review=rewritten; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=231-232

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": resourceVersion: a string that identifies the internal version of this object that can be used by clients to determine when objects have changed. This value MUST be treated as opaque by clients and passed unmodified back to the server. Clients should not assume that the resource version has meaning across namespaces, different kinds of resources, or different servers. (See concurrency control, below, for more details.)
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_021; review=kept; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": generation: a sequence number representing a specific generation of the desired state. Set by the system and monotonically increasing, per-resource. May be compared, such as for RAW and WAW consistency.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_022; review=kept; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have a `metadata.creationTimestamp` field representing the RFC 3339 date and time at which the object was created.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_023; review=rewritten; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": deletionTimestamp: a string representing an RFC 3339 date of the date and time after which this resource will be deleted. This field is set by the server when a graceful deletion is requested by the user, and is not directly settable by a client. The resource will be deleted (no longer visible from resource lists, and not reachable by name) after the time in this field except when the object has a finalizer set. In case the finalizer is set the deletion of the object is postponed at least until the finalizer is removed. Once the deletionTimestamp is set, this value may not be unset or be set further into the future, although it may be shortened or the resource may be deleted prior to this time.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_024; review=kept; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have a `metadata.labels` field containing string key-value pairs used to organize and categorize objects.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_025; review=rewritten; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: Every object SHOULD have a `metadata.annotations` field that external tooling can use to store and retrieve arbitrary metadata about the object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_026; review=rewritten; section=Metadata; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=245-246

constraints.text: The `status` summarizes the current state of the object in the system, and is usually persisted with the object by automated processes but may be generated on the fly.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_027; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: As a general guideline, fields in `status` should be the most recent observations of actual state, but they may contain information such as the results of allocations or similar operations which are executed in response to the object's `spec`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_028; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Types with both `spec` and `status` stanzas can (and usually should) have distinct authorization scopes for them.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_029; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In order to facilitate level-based operation and expression of declarative configuration, fields in the specification should have declarative rather than imperative names and semantics -- they represent the desired state, not actions intended to yield the desired state.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_031; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The PUT and POST verbs on objects MUST ignore the `status` values, to avoid accidentally overwriting the `status` in read-modify-write scenarios.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_032; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: A `/status` subresource MUST be provided to enable system components to update statuses of resources they manage.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_033; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Modification of just part of an object may be achieved by GETting the resource, modifying part of the spec, labels, or annotations, and then PUTting it back.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_034; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some objects may expose alternative resource representations that allow mutation of the status, or performing custom actions on the object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_035; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All objects that represent a physical resource whose state may vary from the user's desired intent SHOULD have a `spec` and a `status`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_036; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Objects whose state cannot vary from the user's desired intent MAY have only `spec`, and MAY rename `spec` to a more appropriate name.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_037; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Objects that contain both `spec` and `status` should not contain additional top-level fields other than the standard metadata fields.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_038; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some objects which are not persisted in the system - such as `SubjectAccessReview` and other webhook style calls - may choose to add `spec` and `status` to encapsulate a "call and response" pattern.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_039; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: For these RPC like objects the only operation may be POST, but having a consistent schema between submission and response reduces the complexity of these clients.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_040; review=kept; section=Spec and Status; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Conditions should complement more detailed information about the observed status of an object written by a controller, rather than replace it.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_041; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Objects may report multiple conditions, and new types of conditions may be added in the future or by 3rd party controllers.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_042; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: This collection should be treated as a map with a key of `type`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_043; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Conditions should be added to explicitly convey properties that users and components care about rather than requiring those properties to be inferred from other observations. Once defined, the meaning of a Condition can not be changed arbitrarily - it becomes part of the API, and has the same backwards- and forwards-compatibility concerns of any other part of the API.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_044; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Controllers should apply their conditions to a resource the first time they visit the resource, even if the `status` is Unknown. This allows other components in the system to know that the condition exists and the controller is making progress on reconciling that resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_045; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Not all controllers will observe the previous advice about reporting "Unknown" or "False" values. For known conditions, the absence of a condition `status` should be interpreted the same as `Unknown`, and typically indicates that reconciliation has not yet finished (or that the resource state may not yet be observable).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_046; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Condition type names should make sense for humans; neither positive nor negative polarity can be recommended as a general rule. A negative condition like "MemoryExhausted" may be easier for humans to understand than "SufficientMemory". Conversely, "Ready" or "Succeeded" may be easier to understand than "Failed", because "Failed=Unknown" or "Failed=False" may cause double-negative confusion.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_047; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Condition type names should describe the current observed state of the resource, rather than describing the current state transitions. This typically means that the name should be an adjective ("Ready", "OutOfDisk") or a past-tense verb ("Succeeded", "Failed") rather than a present-tense verb ("Deploying"). Intermediate states may be indicated by setting the `status` of the condition to `Unknown`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_048; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: For state transitions which take a long period of time (e.g. more than 1 minute), it is reasonable to treat the transition itself as an observed state. In these cases, the Condition (such as "Resizing") itself should not be transient, and should instead be signalled using the `True`/`False`/`Unknown` pattern. This allows other observers to determine the last update from the controller, whether successful or failed. In cases where the state transition is unable to complete and continued reconciliation is not feasible, the Reason and Message should be used to indicate that the transition failed.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_049; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When designing Conditions for a resource, it's helpful to have a common top-level condition which summarizes more detailed conditions. Simple consumers may simply query the top-level condition. Although they are not a consistent standard, the `Ready` and `Succeeded` condition types may be used by API designers for long-running and bounded-execution objects, respectively.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_050; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Conditions should follow the standard schema included in k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1/types.go.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_051; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Conditions should be included as a top level element in `status`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_052; review=rewritten; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Use of the `Reason` field is required.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_054; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Condition types should be named in PascalCase.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_055; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Short condition names are preferred (e.g. "Ready" over "MyResourceReady").
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_056; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Condition `status` values may be `True`, `False`, or `Unknown`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_057; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The absence of a condition should be interpreted the same as `Unknown`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_058; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In general, condition values may change back and forth, but some condition transitions may be monotonic, depending on the resource and condition type.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_059; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The system is level-based rather than edge-triggered, and should assume an Open World.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_060; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The pattern of using `phase` is deprecated.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_061; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Newer API types should use conditions instead.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_062; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In condition types, and everywhere else they appear in the API, `Reason` is intended to be a one-word, CamelCase representation of the category of cause of the current status, and `Message` is intended to be a human-readable phrase or sentence, which may contain specific details of the individual occurrence.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_063; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Status information that may be large (especially proportional in size to collections of other resources, such as lists of references to other objects -- see below) and/or rapidly changing, such as resource usage, should be put into separate objects, with possibly a reference from the original object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_064; review=kept; section=Typical status properties; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In order to ensure that GETs of individual objects remain bounded in time and space, these sets may be queried via separate API queries, but will not be expanded in the referring object's status.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_066; review=kept; section=References to related objects; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: References in the `status` of the referee to the referrer may be permitted, when the references are one-to-one and do not need to be frequently updated, particularly in an edge-based manner.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_067; review=kept; section=References to related objects; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All public integer fields MUST use the Go `int32` or Go `int64` types, not `int` (which is ambiguously sized, depending on target platform). Internal types may use `int`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_068; review=kept; section=Primitive types; kind=bullet

constraints.text: All numbers (e.g. `int32`, `int64`) are converted to `float64` by Javascript and some other languages, so any field which is expected to exceed that either in magnitude or in precision (e.g. integer values > 53 bits) should be serialized and accepted as strings. `int64` fields must be bounds-checked to be within the range of `-(2^53) < x < (2^53)`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_069; review=kept; section=Primitive types; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When an acronym or initialism each letter in the acronym should be uppercase, such as with `ClientIP` or `TCPDelay`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_070; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When a proper name or the name of a command-line executable is used as a constant the proper name should be represented in consistent casing - examples: `systemd`, `iptables`, `IPVS`, `cgroupfs`, `Docker` (as a generic concept), `docker` (as the command-line executable).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_071; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If a proper name is used which has mixed capitalization like `eBPF` that should be preserved in a longer constant such as `eBPFDelegation`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_072; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All API within Kubernetes must leverage constants in this style, including flags and configuration files.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_073; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Where inconsistent constants were previously used, new flags should be CamelCase only, and over time old flags should be updated to accept a CamelCase value alongside the inconsistent constant.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_074; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If new values are added to the flag, both forms should be supported.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_076; review=kept; section=Constants; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All fields in the set should be Optional.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_077; review=kept; section=Unions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Sometimes, when a new type is created, the api designer may anticipate that a union will be needed in the future, even if only one field is allowed initially.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_078; review=kept; section=Unions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In this case, be sure to make the field Optional In the validation, you may still return an error if the sole field is unset.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_079; review=kept; section=Unions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Every list or simple kind SHOULD have the following metadata in a nested object field called "metadata": resourceVersion: a string that identifies the common version of the objects returned by in a list. This value MUST be treated as opaque by clients and passed unmodified back to the server. A resource version is only valid within a single namespace on a single kind of resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_080; review=kept; section=Lists and Simple kinds; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=649-650

constraints.text: Every simple kind returned by the server, and any simple kind sent to the server that must support idempotency or optimistic concurrency should return this value.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_081; review=kept; section=Lists and Simple kinds; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Since simple resources are often used as input alternate actions that modify objects, the resource version of the simple resource should correspond to the resource version of the object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_082; review=kept; section=Lists and Simple kinds; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: An API may represent a single entity in different ways for different clients, or transform an object after certain transitions in the system occur.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_083; review=kept; section=Differing Representations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In these cases, one request object may have two representations available as different resources, or different kinds.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_084; review=kept; section=Differing Representations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: As another example, a "pod status" resource may accept a PUT with the "pod" kind, with different rules about what fields may be changed.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_085; review=kept; section=Differing Representations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Future versions of Kubernetes may allow alternative encodings of objects beyond JSON.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_086; review=kept; section=Differing Representations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: GET /<resourceNamePlural> - Retrieve a list of type <resourceName>, e.g. GET /pods returns a list of Pods.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_087; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: POST /<resourceNamePlural> - Create a new resource from the JSON object provided by the client.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_088; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: GET /<resourceNamePlural>/<name> - Retrieves a single resource with the given name, e.g. GET /pods/first returns a Pod named 'first'. Should be constant time, and the resource should be bounded in size.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_089; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: DELETE /<resourceNamePlural>/<name> - Delete the single resource with the given name. DeleteOptions may specify gracePeriodSeconds, the optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Individual kinds may declare fields which provide a default grace period, and different kinds may have differing kind-wide default grace periods. A user provided grace period overrides a default grace period, including the zero grace period ("now").
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_090; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: DELETE /<resourceNamePlural> - Deletes a list of type <resourceName>, e.g. DELETE /pods a list of Pods.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_091; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: PUT /<resourceNamePlural>/<name> - Update or create the resource with the given name with the JSON object provided by the client. Whether a resource can be created with a PUT request depends on the particular resource's storage strategy configuration, specifically the `AllowCreateOnUpdate()` return value. Most built-in types do not allow this.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_092; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: PATCH /<resourceNamePlural>/<name> - Selectively modify the specified fields of the resource. See more information below.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_093; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: API resources should use the traditional REST pattern: GET /<resourceNamePlural>?watch=true - Receive a stream of JSON objects corresponding to changes made to any resource of the given kind over time.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_094; review=kept; section=Verbs on Resources; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=688-688

constraints.text: Resource implementers can optionally include "short names" and categories in the discovery information published for a resource type, which clients may use as hints when resolving ambiguous user invocations.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_095; review=kept; section=Short-names and Categories; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: "Short names" listed in discovery may be used by clients as hints to resolve ambiguous user invocations to a single resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_096; review=kept; section=Short-names; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Short-name matches may be given lower priority than an exact match of a resource type, so use of short names increases potential for inconsistent behavior in clusters with custom resources installed, if those custom resource types overlap with short names.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_097; review=kept; section=Short-names; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Categories listed in discovery may be used by clients as hints to resolve user invocations to multiple resources.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_098; review=kept; section=Categories; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All compatible Kubernetes APIs MUST support "name idempotency" and respond with an HTTP status code 409 when a request is made to POST an object that has the same name as an existing object in the system.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_099; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Names generated by the system may be requested using `metadata.generateName`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_100; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: GenerateName indicates that the name should be made unique by the server prior to persisting it.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_101; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: A non-empty value for the field indicates the server should attempt to make the name unique (and the name returned to the client will be different than the name passed).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_102; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The provided value must be valid within the rules for Name, and may be truncated by the length of the suffix.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_103; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If this field is specified, and Name is not present, the server will return a 409 with Reason `AlreadyExists` if the generated name exists, and the client should retry (after waiting at least the amount of time indicated in the Retry-After header, if it is present).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_104; review=kept; section=Idempotency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Fields must be either optional or required.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_105; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: A field that is required means that the writer must express an opinion about the value of the field.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_106; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: An optional field is one where the writer may choose to omit the field entirely.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_107; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Readers should not assume that the field is present, unless the field also has a server-side default value.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_108; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The API server should allow POSTing and PUTing a resource with this field unset.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_109; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When the field type has a built-in `nil` value, such as a map or a slice, and your use case means that you need to be able to distinguish between "field not set" and "field set to an empty value", you should use a pointer to the type, even though it has a built-in `nil` value.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_110; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: New fields should explicitly set either an `+optional` or `+required` comment tag.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_112; review=rewritten; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The API server should not allow POSTing or PUTing a resource with this field unset.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_114; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=bullet

constraints.text: They _typically_ do not use pointer types in the Go definition (e.g. `AnotherFlag SomeFlag`), though required fields where the zero value is a valid value must use pointer types, paired with an `omitempty` struct tag to avoid spurious null serializations.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_115; review=kept; section=Optional vs. Required; kind=bullet

constraints.text: For `bool` types, the zero value is `false`, which is always a valid user choice. `bool` types should always be pointers, and should always use the `omitempty` tag.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_117; review=kept; section=Serialization of optional/required fields; kind=bullet

constraints.text: In these cases, not using `omitempty` provides the same result, but pollutes the marshaled object with zero values and is not recommended.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_119; review=kept; section=Serialization of optional/required fields; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Required structs should use `omitzero` to avoid marshalling the zero value.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_121; review=kept; section=Serialization of optional/required fields; kind=bullet

constraints.text: In some cases, these values may be conditional on or deterministically derived from other fields (e.g. "if otherField is X then this field defaults to 0" or "this field defaults to the value of otherField").
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_124; review=kept; section=Static Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The static defaulting logic is unaware of updates and has no concept of "previous value", which means this inter-field relationship becomes the user's problem - they must update both the field they care about and the "other" field.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_126; review=kept; section=Static Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In very rare cases, these values may be allocated from some pool or determined by some other method (e.g. Service's IP and IP-family related fields need to consider other configuration settings).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_127; review=kept; section=Static Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Static defaults are the best choice for values which are logically required, but which have a value that works well for most users.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_128; review=kept; section=Static Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Static defaulting must not consider any state except the object being operated upon (and the complexity of Service API stands as an example of why).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_129; review=kept; section=Static Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Unlike static defaults, these may consider external state (such as annotations on StorageClass objects) when deciding default values, and must handle things like race conditions (e.g. a StorageClass is designated the default, but the admission controller has not yet seen that update).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_132; review=kept; section=Admission Controlled Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: As such, fields which are initialized this way must be strictly optional.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_133; review=kept; section=Admission Controlled Defaults; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Like admission controlled defaults, these controllers may consider external state when deciding what values to set, must handle race conditions, and can be disabled.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_134; review=kept; section=Controller-Assigned Defaults (aka Late Initialization); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Fields which are initialized this way must be strictly optional (meaning observers will see the object without these fields set, and that is allowable and semantically correct).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_135; review=kept; section=Controller-Assigned Defaults (aka Late Initialization); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Like all controllers, care must be taken to not clobber unrelated fields or values (e.g. in an array).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_136; review=kept; section=Controller-Assigned Defaults (aka Late Initialization); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Using one of the patch or apply mechanisms is recommended to facilitate composition and concurrency of controllers.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_137; review=kept; section=Controller-Assigned Defaults (aka Late Initialization); kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All forms of defaulting should only set previously unset fields.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_138; review=rewritten; section=What May Be Defaulted; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=1048-1048

constraints.text: All forms of defaulting should only add keys to maps.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_139; review=rewritten; section=What May Be Defaulted; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=1048-1048

constraints.text: All forms of defaulting should only add values to arrays with mergeable semantics (`+listType=map` tag or `patchStrategy:"merge"` attribute in the type definition).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_140; review=rewritten; section=What May Be Defaulted; kind=bullet; lead_in_span=1048-1048

constraints.text: If they requested something invalid, they should get an error.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_141; review=kept; section=What May Be Defaulted; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The goal is that, for a given input (e.g. YAML file), PUT on an existing object should produce the same result as if you used that input to create the object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_142; review=kept; section=Considerations For PUT Operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Calling PUT a second time with the same input should be idempotent and should not change the resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_143; review=kept; section=Considerations For PUT Operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When adding a field with a static or admission controlled default, this must be considered.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_146; review=kept; section=Considerations For PUT Operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: However, it's important to note that the application should *not* rely on the implementation details of the versioning system maintained by Kubernetes.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_148; review=kept; section=Concurrency Control and Consistency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: This value MUST be treated as opaque by clients and passed unmodified back to the server.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_150; review=kept; section=Concurrency Control and Consistency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Clients should not assume that the resource version has meaning across namespaces, different kinds of resources, or different servers.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_151; review=kept; section=Concurrency Control and Consistency; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: APIs may return alternative representations of any resource in response to an Accept header or under alternative endpoints, but the default serialization for input and output of API responses MUST be JSON.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_154; review=kept; section=Serialization Format; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All dates should be serialized as RFC3339 strings.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_155; review=kept; section=Serialization Format; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Units must either be explicit in the field name (e.g., `timeoutSeconds`), or must be specified as part of the value (e.g., `resource.Quantity`).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_156; review=kept; section=Units; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Which approach is preferred is TBD, though currently we use the `fooSeconds` convention for durations.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_157; review=kept; section=Units; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Duration fields must be represented as integer fields with units being part of the field name (e.g. `leaseDurationSeconds`).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_158; review=kept; section=Units; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some APIs may need to identify which field in a JSON object is invalid, or to reference a value to extract from a separate resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_159; review=kept; section=Selecting Fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Object references on a namespaced type should usually refer only to objects in the same namespace.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_160; review=kept; section=Object references; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If a non-built-in types chooses to have cross-namespace references the semantics of the edge cases above should be clearly described and the permissions issues should be resolved.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_162; review=kept; section=Object references; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The name of the reference field should be of the format "{field}Ref", with "Ref" always included in the suffix.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_163; review=kept; section=Naming of the reference field; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The "{field}" component should be named to indicate the purpose of the reference.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_164; review=kept; section=Naming of the reference field; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In the case of a list of object references, the field should be of the format "{field}Refs", with the same guidance as the singular case above.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_165; review=kept; section=Naming of the reference field; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Controllers should assume that a version of a resource may change, and include appropriate error handling.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_166; review=kept; section=Referencing resources with multiple versions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Controllers should be authored with the assumption that the referenced resource may not exist, and include error handling to make the issue clear to the user.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_168; review=kept; section=Handling of resources that do not exist; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: As the permissions of the controller can differ from the permissions of the author of the object the controller is managing, it is possible that the author of the object may not have permissions to view the referred object.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_169; review=kept; section=Minimize copying or printing values to the referrer object; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: This schema should only be used when the intention is to always have the reference only be to a single resource.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_172; review=kept; section=Single resource reference; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If an object reference may be used to reference resources of arbitrary types and the mapping between kind and resource could be ambiguous, "resource" should be used in the object reference.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_174; review=kept; section=Kind vs. Resource; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: However, as schemas can vary across different versions of a resource, the controller must also handle these differences.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_175; review=kept; section=Controller behavior; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: As any retrievable field would be common to all objects, any version of the resource should do.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_176; review=kept; section=Controller behavior; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Since the schema of an object can differ for different versions of a resource, this means that a “version” is required for this type of reference.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_177; review=kept; section=Field reference; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The fieldPath should point to a single value, and use the recommended field selector notation to denote the field path.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_178; review=kept; section=Field reference; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: In this scenario, the user will supply all of the required path elements: group, version, resource, name, and possibly namespace.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_179; review=kept; section=Controller behavior; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Clients SHOULD handle these types of objects when appropriate.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_184; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When fields in the status object have the same meaning as generally defined HTTP headers and that header is returned with the response, the header should be considered as having higher priority.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_185; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: `message` may contain a human-readable description of the error.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_186; review=rewritten; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: `reason` may contain a machine-readable, one-word, CamelCase description of why this operation is in the `Failure` status.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_187; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: `details` may contain extended data associated with the reason.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_188; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Each reason may define its own extended details.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_189; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The server should set the `Retry-After` HTTP header and return `retryAfterSeconds` in the details field of the object. A value of `0` is the default.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_196; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=bullet

constraints.text: The server should set the `Retry-After` HTTP header and return `retryAfterSeconds` in the details field of the object. A value of `0` is the default.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_197; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Http status code: `500 StatusInternalServerError` `code` may contain the suggested HTTP return code for this status.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_198; review=kept; section=Response Status Kind; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Generate events for situations users or administrators should be alerted about.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_199; review=kept; section=Events; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Go field names must be PascalCase. JSON field names must be camelCase. Other than capitalization of the initial letter, the two should almost always match. No underscores or dashes in either.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_200; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Field and resource names should be declarative, not imperative (SomethingDoer, DoneBy, DoneAt).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_201; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: `FooController` is a deprecated kind naming convention. Name the kind after the thing being controlled instead (e.g., `Job` rather than `JobController`).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_202; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: The name of a field that specifies the time at which `something` occurs should be called `somethingTime`. Do not use `stamp` (e.g., `creationTimestamp`).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_203; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: `fooPeriodSeconds` is preferred for periodic intervals and other waiting periods (e.g., over `fooIntervalSeconds`).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_204; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: `fooTimeoutSeconds` is preferred for inactivity/unresponsiveness deadlines.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_205; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: `fooDeadlineSeconds` is preferred for activity completion deadlines.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_206; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Acronyms should similarly only be used when extremely commonly known. All letters in the acronym should have the same case, using the appropriate case for the situation. For example, at the beginning of a field name, the acronym should be all lowercase, such as "httpGet". Where used as a constant, all letters should be uppercase, such as "TCP" or "UDP".
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_207; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: The name of a field referring to another resource of kind `Foo` by name should be called `fooName`. The name of a field referring to another resource of kind `Foo` by ObjectReference (or subset thereof) should be called `fooRef`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_208; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: The name of a field expressing a boolean property called 'fooable' should be called `Fooable`, not `IsFooable`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_209; review=kept; section=Naming conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: The name of a namespace must be a DNS_LABEL.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_210; review=kept; section=Namespace Names; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Users will use labels to select resources to operate on, display label values in CLI/UI columns, etc. Users should always retain full power and flexibility over the label schemas they apply to labels in their namespaces.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_211; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: However, we should support conveniences for common cases by default.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_212; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Users should always be able to turn off auto-generation, in order to permit some of the scenarios described above.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_214; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: However, I firmly believe that the user should always be allowed to select the label keys to use on their resources, so it should always be possible to override default label keys.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_215; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Therefore, resources supporting auto-generation of unique labels should have a `uniqueLabelKey` field, so that the user could specify the key if they wanted to, but if unspecified, it could be set by default, such as to the resource type, like job, deployment, or replicationController.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_216; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Annotations may carry arbitrary payloads, including JSON documents.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_217; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Third-party components must use prefixed keys.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_218; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Key prefixes under the "kubernetes.io" and "k8s.io" domains are reserved for use by the kubernetes project and must not be used by third-parties.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_219; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: This pattern is deprecated.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_220; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some such annotations may still exist, but no new annotations may be defined.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_221; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Key names should be all lowercase, with words separated by dashes instead of camelCase.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_222; review=rewritten; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Unprefixed keys are reserved for end-users. All other labels and annotations must be prefixed.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_223; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Such keys are effectively part of the kubernetes API and may be subject to deprecation and compatibility policies.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_224; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: "kubernetes.io" is the preferred form for labels and annotations, "k8s.io" should not be used for new map keys.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_225; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Key names, including prefixes, should be precise enough that a user could plausibly understand where it came from and what it is for.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_226; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Key prefixes should carry as much context as possible.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_227; review=kept; section=Label, selector, and annotation conventions; kind=bullet

constraints.text: Clients should use the SPDY protocols if their clients have native support, or WebSockets as a fallback.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_229; review=kept; section=WebSockets and SPDY; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Note that WebSockets is susceptible to Head-of-Line blocking and so clients must read and process each message sequentially.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_230; review=kept; section=WebSockets and SPDY; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: For all new APIs, declarative validation is the preferred approach for the validation rules it supports.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_231; review=kept; section=Validation; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All fields should be declared optional or required, and validation should check the requiredness.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_232; review=kept; section=Requiredness; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Almost all string fields should be checked for format.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_233; review=kept; section=String fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Some format checks include checking for length (e.g. "short-name", aka, "DNS Label" must be less than 64 bytes).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_234; review=kept; section=String fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All string fields should be checked for maximum length.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_235; review=kept; section=String fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If the field has a minimum length, that should also be checked.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_236; review=kept; section=String fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If validation treats "abc" and "ABC" as the same, then ALL consumers of that field must do the same, and it is very easy to miss a case.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_237; review=kept; section=String fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All numeric fields should be bounds-checked, both for too-small values (including negative) and for too-large values.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_238; review=kept; section=Numeric fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All list fields should be checked for maximum size.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_239; review=kept; section=List fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If the list has a minimum size, that should also be checked.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_240; review=kept; section=List fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All list fields should have their "listType" tag(s) set.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_241; review=kept; section=List fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Lists with set or map semantics should be checked for uniqueness (declarative validation will do this authomatically).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_242; review=kept; section=List fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: All map fields should be checked for maximum size.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_243; review=kept; section=Map fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: If the map has a minimum size, that should also be checked.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_244; review=kept; section=Map fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Map fields should have their keys validated, in addition to their values.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_245; review=kept; section=Map fields; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When asserting a requirement in the positive, use "must". Examples: "must be greater than 0", "must match regex '[a-z]+'". Words like "should" imply that the assertion is optional, and must be avoided.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_246; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When asserting a formatting requirement in the negative, use "must not". Example: "must not contain '..'". Words like "should not" imply that the assertion is optional, and must be avoided.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_247; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When asserting a behavioral requirement in the negative, use "may not". Examples: "may not be specified when otherField is empty", "only `name` may be specified".
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_248; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When referencing a literal string value, indicate the literal in single-quotes. Example: "must not contain '..'".
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_249; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When referencing another field name, indicate the name in back-quotes. Example: "must be greater than \`request\`".
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_250; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When specifying inequalities, use words rather than symbols. Examples: "must be less than 256", "must be greater than or equal to 0". Do not use words like "larger than", "bigger than", "more than", "higher than", etc.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_251; review=kept; section=Error messages; kind=bullet

constraints.text: A set of N fields, only one of which should be set at any given time - effectively a union.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_252; review=kept; section=Automatic Resource Allocation And Deallocation; kind=bullet

constraints.text: When the user chooses to change the `discriminator` value (e.g., from `Type X` to `Type Y`) without changing any other fields then the system should clear the fields that were used to represent `Type X` in the union along with releasing resources that were attached to `Type X`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_253; review=kept; section=Automatic Resource Allocation And Deallocation; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: This should automatically happen irrespective of how these values and resources were allocated (i.e., reserved by the user or automatically allocated by the system.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_254; review=kept; section=Automatic Resource Allocation And Deallocation; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Sometimes the user can request a specific value and a controller must confirm or reject that request.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_255; review=kept; section=Representing Allocated Values; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: The common theme among all of these is that the system should not trust users with such fields, and must verify or otherwise confirm such requests before using them.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_256; review=kept; section=Representing Allocated Values; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: New APIs should almost never do this.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_262; review=kept; section=When to use a `spec` field; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Instead, new APIs should use `status`.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_263; review=rewritten; section=When to use a `spec` field; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Since almost everything is happening asynchronously to almost everything else, controller implementations should take care around the ordering of operations.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_264; review=kept; section=Sequencing operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: However, if it would be problematic for a client to observe the `status` value before it is actuated then the controller must actuate first and update `status` afterward.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_265; review=kept; section=Sequencing operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: Controllers must take care to consider how a `status` field will be handled in the case of interrupted control loops (e.g. controller crash and restart), and must act idempotently and consistently.
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_266; review=kept; section=Sequencing operations; kind=paragraph_sentence

constraints.text: When using this pattern, you must account for lifecycle of the allocated objects (who cleans them up and when) as well as the "linkage" between them and the main type (often using the same name, an object-ref field, or a selector).
constraints.notes: source_rule_id=kw_norm_267; review=kept; section=When to use a different type; kind=paragraph_sentence
